The contribution claim as stated is self-contradicting and requires reconstruction before any strengthening is meaningful.","R1.2":"This is load-bearing: the core logical arc (value-based insight → sample efficiency gain) is broken because the technical procedure describes policy gradient, not value-based RL. No replay-buffer Q-learning, no Bellman backup, no explicit Q-function — yet these are the claimed mechanisms. The inconsistency invalidates the paper's central argument.","R1.3":"Result claims ('expert-level performance', 'competitive performance', 'superior sample efficiency') are asserted without quantitative grounding — no scores, no confidence intervals, no learning curves referenced. Strengthening requires tightening evidence-to-claim bindings.","R1.4":"Comparisons are made only against 'standard policy-gradient baselines,' never against value-based methods (SAC, TD3, IQL) that represent the natural reference class for the claimed contribution. The novelty delta cannot be assessed without this comparison.","R1.6":"'Make this stronger' plausibly targets rhetorical force and prose precision; multiple sentences are hedged to the point of vagueness ('competitive,' 'more sample-efficient than approaches that only model') and the algorithm paragraph reads as a procedure list rather than a motivated narrative."},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Does 'stronger' mean primarily improving rhetorical and prose quality (R1.6), or does it include fixing substantive technical and logical issues — specifically, the mismatch between claiming a value-based algorithm and describing a policy-gradient one (R1.2)? These require fundamentally different interventions."}

rationale.R1.1: The thesis frames QMaster as 'value-based' via explicit action-value estimation, but the described algorithm is REINFORCE with a learned baseline — a policy-gradient method. The contribution claim as stated is self-contradicting and requires reconstruction before any strengthening is meaningful.
rationale.R1.2: This is load-bearing: the core logical arc (value-based insight → sample efficiency gain) is broken because the technical procedure describes policy gradient, not value-based RL. No replay-buffer Q-learning, no Bellman backup, no explicit Q-function — yet these are the claimed mechanisms. The inconsistency invalidates the paper's central argument.
rationale.R1.3: Result claims ('expert-level performance', 'competitive performance', 'superior sample efficiency') are asserted without quantitative grounding — no scores, no confidence intervals, no learning curves referenced. Strengthening requires tightening evidence-to-claim bindings.
rationale.R1.4: Comparisons are made only against 'standard policy-gradient baselines,' never against value-based methods (SAC, TD3, IQL) that represent the natural reference class for the claimed contribution. The novelty delta cannot be assessed without this comparison.
rationale.R1.6: 'Make this stronger' plausibly targets rhetorical force and prose precision; multiple sentences are hedged to the point of vagueness ('competitive,' 'more sample-efficient than approaches that only model') and the algorithm paragraph reads as a procedure list rather than a motivated narrative.

parsed.clarification_question: Does 'stronger' mean primarily improving rhetorical and prose quality (R1.6), or does it include fixing substantive technical and logical issues — specifically, the mismatch between claiming a value-based algorithm and describing a policy-gradient one (R1.2)? These require fundamentally different interventions.
